exemplar.title:
To what extent have the regions of Siemens Campus and
Roethelheimpark used environmentally sustainable urban development
strategies?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and effectively, with clear section headings and consistent focus on the research question.
Research question is explicitly stated, focused, and maintained throughout the investigation.
Methodology is systematically described with detailed sampling procedures and reproducible scoring rubrics.
weaknesses: Introductory narrative at times is generic and wordy, with limited engagement with specific academic debates.
Scope of the research question covers six indicators equally, stretching depth of analysis.
Some methodological limitations (singleâday sampling, single observer, partial map data) are acknowledged but not fully mitigated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates clear and effective communication of the topic and a wellâfocused research question, supported by a largely appropriate mixedâmethods design. Minor generic language in the introduction and some unaddressed methodological constraints prevent a topâband score, placing this criterion firmly in the upper end of the 3â4 band and into band 5.

strengths: Sound grasp of smartâgrowth theory and urban sustainability concepts applied accurately to the case studies.
Terminology such as âwalkability,â âmixed land use,â and GIS/transport terms are used correctly and consistently.
Effective use of an authoritative diagram of Smart Growth principles and definitions from Smart Growth America.
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on web sources with limited engagement with higherâlevel academic literature.
Explanations of ecological and socioâeconomic contexts remain largely descriptive rather than analytical.
Occasional imprecision in terminology (e.g. conflation of SGD/SDG) and some colloquial phrasing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear subject knowledge and generally accurate use of key concepts, meriting a solid performance in the 3â4 band. Limitations in source quality and depth of analysis prevent advancement to the highest band.

strengths: Data collection tools (systematic sampling, GIS mapping, radar charts) are consistently relevant and well applied to the research question.
Conclusions are supported by visualizations and linked to evidence in a mostly coherent comparative argument.
Discussion acknowledges anomalies and methodological limitations, demonstrating critical awareness.
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive, with no inferential statistics to test significance of differences.
Some evaluation of counter-arguments and deeper theoretical integration is limited.
Subjective scoring methods and missing data (ân/aâ) handling are not fully clarified.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation shows a good level of analysis and evaluation with clear relevance to the research question and a generally coherent argument. However, the absence of inferential testing, reliance on descriptive conclusions, and limited depth of critical synthesis constrain it to the upper half of the 4â6 band.

strengths: Report structure is clearly appropriate: logical sequencing, numbered sections, and a complete table of contents.
Layout conventions (headings, numbered figures and tables, title page, word count, page numbers) are consistently applied.
Maps, tables, and photographs are effectively integrated to support the narrative.
weaknesses: Some figure captions are overly verbose and could be streamlined for readability.
Minor typographical inconsistencies and occasional imbalance in section lengths slightly interrupt flow.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation fully meets IB expectations with a coherent structure and clean layout. Only minor caption verbosity and small typographical issues prevent an otherwise flawless execution, justifying the top mark in this criterion.

strandSelections.reasoning: Primary data are generally appropriate and clearly linked to the research question.  Systematic sampling, scoring criteria and GIS mapping demonstrate a conscious attempt to apply research tools.  Data are tabulated, averaged and compared through graphs and radar charts, showing a good level of organisation.  Some methodological weaknesses (subjective scoring, single-observer bias, limited temporal coverage) reduce the overall rigour, but the research remains clearly relevant and largely sufficient to address the RQ.  This corresponds to the middle of the 4-6 band for research application.

The student outlines an explicit, largely appropriate mixed-methods design: systematic field sampling, scoring sheets derived from secondary smart-growth criteria, and limited GIS/land-use analysis.  Primary data collection process (time, dates, number of points) is described and appendices provide scoring rubrics.  Nevertheless, some methodological choices (e.g. single-day, midday survey; subjective scoring by one observer; partial inaccessibility of Siemens Campus; reliance on possibly outdated land-use map) are acknowledged but not adequately mitigated.  Source use is mainly websites and maps with little evidence of academic literature.  Hence the methodology is âmostly complete with generally relevant methodsâ rather than fully appropriate and comprehensive.

The essay shows a sound grasp of smart-growth theory, urban sustainability concepts (walkability, bikeability, mixed-use, etc.) and applies them to the chosen sites.  Descriptions of Erlangenâs urban development, brownfield redevelopment and transport networks indicate competent geographic understanding.  However, discussion of ecological processes and urban planning frameworks relies heavily on web sources and lacks engagement with higher-level academic literature.  Some explanations remain descriptive rather than analytical, and regional context (e.g. socio-economic factors) is only briefly addressed.  Overall, the knowledge is clear but not consistently sophisticated, aligning with the 3-4 band.

Appropriate academic conventions are generally observed: consistent use of headings, tables and figures numbered and captioned, word count and title stated, pages appear numbered, and overall formatting is clear.  Maps and photographs are integrated, though some captions are verbose and referencing below images is irregular.  Minor typographical issues (e.g., extra spaces, duplicated figure titles) and dense visual layouts occasionally hinder readability, but layout remains acceptable and supports communication of ideas.  These features align with the 3-4 band; a score of 3 recognises good but not flawless application.

Key terms such as âSmart Growth Development,â âwalkability,â and âmixed land useâ are defined early and are generally applied correctly throughout the essay.  The student uses GIS, land-use and transport terminology with reasonable accuracy.  Occasional imprecision (e.g. confusing âSGD Theoryâ with âSDGâ) and some colloquial phrasing (âscore better,â âbiking availabilityâ) detract from consistency, and referencing of models beyond Smart Growth is limited.  Nonetheless, the terminology is mostly accurate and shows understanding, meriting the 3â4 band.

The title, abstract and opening paragraphs make the geographical focus (environmental sustainability of two named districts in Erlangen) immediately evident and the purpose of comparison is reiterated throughout the essay and section headings.  The student signals why the topic matters (sustainable urban development, smart-growth principles) and consistently links sections back to the guiding idea of âto what extentâ¦â.  Although clear, some elements of the introductory narrative are wordy and occasionally repetitive, and the explanation of academic significance remains rather generic (e.g. references to âpeople are aware of sustainabilityâ).  Nonetheless, the reader can follow the line of inquiry without difficulty, showing adequate-to-good clarity and focus.

The essay maintains a logical comparative structure (criterion-by-criterion, region-by-region), enabling an overall argument that Roethelheimpark exhibits greater sustainability.  Claims are generally backed by data and illustrative evidence.  Nevertheless, the discussion often slips into narrative description, and synthesis across criteria is limited to the brief conclusion.  Counter-arguments (e.g., Siemens Campusâs potential upon completion) are acknowledged but not deeply explored.  The argument is coherent and mostly effective but lacks the depth and critical weighting characteristic of higher bands, so a solid 4 within the 4-6 band is justified.

The student carries out basic statistical treatment (means) and uses visualisations (scatter and radar graphs) to interpret patterns.  Explanations link results to smart-growth principles and physical evidence (photos, maps).  Some evaluation of anomalies (e.g., high scores in Modules 1&2, time-of-day effects) is present.  However, many conclusions remain descriptive; inferential statistics are absent, and the significance of differences (e.g., 0.1 score gap in transport) is not critically tested.  The evaluation of limitations is sound but not fully integrated into the argument.  Thus analysis is âadequate with mostly supported conclusions,â fitting the lower end of the 4-6 band.

The report follows IB EE conventions: title page, table of contents, clearly numbered sections (Introduction, Methodology, Analysis, Conclusion, Evaluation, References, Appendix).  Headings and sub-headings guide the reader and there is logical progression from method to analysis to conclusion.  Minor issues existâsome repetition, occasional imbalance between sections, and lengthy figure captions embedded in the main text break reading flowâbut overall the structure supports understanding.  These qualities meet the description of the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects the noted inconsistencies.

The research question is explicitly stated, focused on two precise case-study areas and on the measurable concept of environmentally sustainable urban development.  The wording "to what extent" invites investigation and evaluation.  However, the scope remains fairly broad because six different smart-growth indicators are treated in equal depth, stretching the analysis and limiting depth for each.  Some ancillary hypotheses are posed, but they mirror the RQ rather than add nuance.  Overall, the question is clear and mostly focused, fulfilling the descriptors of the 3â4 band.

annotations.comment: The opening sentences are generic and anecdotal; consider refining to situate the study within specific academic debates and scholarly contexts for stronger clarity of purpose.
The student correctly includes the word count at the start of the report, fulfilling the IB requirement and aiding transparency in document length management.
Restating the research question in italicized introduction helps maintain focus and signals its centrality to the investigationâs structure.
This contextual paragraph is verbose and makes broad assertions without supporting citations; tighten language and integrate scholarly references to enhance academic rigor.
The chronological overview of the Siemens Campus development is clear and provides necessary methodological context for later comparative analysis.
Figure 3 lacks a visible scale bar and north arrow, which are essential for map interpretation; include these elements to meet academic mapping standards.
Table 1 presents raw data in a clear, wellâorganized format, facilitating straightforward interpretation of primary observations.
The multiâscale GIS map (Fig. 2) effectively situates the case study areas, demonstrating strong application of spatial research tools.
Case study descriptions rely heavily on lengthy quotations from websites without critical analysis; engage more deeply with the source material to show advanced subject understanding.
The research question is prominently stated in the title section, providing clear focus and guiding the reader from the outset.
Figure 1âs caption repeats lengthy descriptions rather than a concise title and source; simplify captions and reserve detailed alt text for appendices to improve readability.
The diagram of Smart Growth principles is effectively labeled and clearly presented, demonstrating strong subject understanding and supporting conceptual clarity.
The hypotheses align directly with the research question and outline clear comparative expectations, supporting methodological focus.
Hypotheses restate the research question without specifying measurable indicators or expected effect sizes, limiting their analytical value.
The radar chart comparison synthesizes multiple criteria into a single visual, facilitating clear critical comparison between regions.
Criteria for Walkability and Biking are referenced in appendices but not summarized in the main methodology, hindering reader understanding of scoring application.
The methodology clearly outlines systematic sampling procedures and records data collection timing, enhancing reproducibility of the investigation.
Limiting primary data collection to a single midday session risks temporal bias; consider multiple sampling periods to mitigate this error.
Citing Smart Growth America definitions demonstrates engagement with authoritative sources and supports accurate use of core concepts.
The mixed land use scoring conflates area size and landâuse diversity without clear justification; clarify rationale to strengthen analytical rigour.
Figure 4âs map of sampling locations is wellâintegrated and visibly distinguishes between case study areas, supporting clear presentation of methodology.
Figure 12âs combination of field photographs and mapped greenspace provides robust, multiâlayered evidence of park design and accessibility.
Collapsing mixed land use scores into a single regional value is a reasonable adaptation for small study areas, simplifying comparative analysis.
The caption for Table 1 lacks a description of score scales, requiring readers to refer back to appendices; include scale context in each table caption.
The SC raw data table includes ân/aâ entries without explanation; explicitly state how missing data were handled to maintain methodological transparency.
The scatter plot for Roethelheimpark is clearly designed, showing score distribution effectively and supporting dataâdriven discussion.
The analysis narrative omits any statistical testing to evaluate the significance of observed differences; consider basic inferential statistics to strengthen conclusions.
Figure 6âs scatter plot does not label all twelve data points explicitly, making it difficult to interpret missing or outlier values without table crossâreference.
Some photo captions are overly verbose and buried in alt text; instead use concise figure titles and place detailed notes in appendix for clarity.
The transport discussion describes proximity qualitatively but omits numerical walking distances and service frequencies; quantify these metrics to enhance analytical precision.
The discussion of results largely restates data observations without integrating wider smart growth theory, limiting depth of critical evaluation.
Linking discussion points directly to field photographs (Figs 8 & 9) strengthens the argument by tying visual evidence to analysis.
Including author photographs enriches empirical depth and illustrates realâworld conditions, demonstrating effective application of research tools.
